title: How bodywork can trigger flashbacks and what to do about it
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You went to feel better. Instead you felt worse. 

 You booked a massage because your shoulders felt like concrete. Or you tried a yoga class because ever...
htmlContent:
You went to feel better. Instead you felt worse.

You booked a massage because your shoulders felt like concrete. Or you tried a yoga class because everyone said it would help with anxiety. Or maybe you finally went to that bodyworker your friend swore by. And somewhere in the middle of it, something went wrong. Your heart started racing. Your vision got weird. You felt a wave of nausea or dread or something you can't name. You might have even dissociated, floating somewhere above your body while some stranger's hands pressed into muscles you'd rather forget exist.

Here's the thing. This is not your fault. And you're not broken because your body responded this way.

I've been there. I remember lying on a massage table, trying to breathe, while my nervous system screamed at me like a smoke detector with no off switch. The therapist kept saying "just relax" and I wanted to scream. Relax? My body was in full combat mode. Every touch felt like a threat. Every breath felt like drowning.

So let's talk about why bodywork can trigger flashbacks. And more , what the hell you can do about it.

Why your body doesn't know the difference between healing hands and harm

Your nervous system is not stupid. It's actually incredibly smart. It's just smart in ways that don't always match your conscious intentions.

Here's what happens. When you've experienced trauma, your body learns that touch can mean danger. Your nervous system builds a database of threat signals. And this database doesn't have a "good touch" versus "bad touch" category. It has a "touch" category. Period. So when a stranger's hands start pressing into your back, your system runs a threat assessment. And if you have unresolved trauma, that assessment often comes back with one word: RUN.

Dr. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, explains this better than anyone. He talks about how our nervous system constantly scans for cues of safety and danger. This process happens below conscious awareness. You don't decide to feel safe or unsafe. Your body decides for you. And when your body detects something that looks like a threat even if your mind knows it's not your system will mobilize for survival.

Think about it. If someone touched you in a way that hurt you before, your body learned a lesson. That lesson was: touch can lead to pain. Your body is trying to protect you from that pain again. It's not trying to ruin your massage. It's trying to keep you alive.

Right? Stay with me here.

The problem is that many bodywork modalities don't account for this. They assume everyone's nervous system can handle the same pressure, the same pace, the same approach. They hand you a waiver and tell you to breathe. But breathing doesn't override a nervous system that's decided you're in danger. Breathing is not a reset button for trauma.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote extensively about how trauma lives in the body, put it this way: the body keeps the score. And when you start working directly with the body, the score starts playing. Loudly. Sometimes deafeningly. That's what a flashback during bodywork is. It's your body playing back an old recording that you didn't even know was still on the tape.

The specific ways bodywork can trigger flashbacks (and what that actually feels like)

Let me be specific about what this looks like, because I think we need to name it clearly. No more vague language about "discomfort" or "emotional release." We're talking about flashbacks here.

Bodywork can trigger flashbacks in several distinct ways:

The position trigger. Lying face down on a table with someone standing over you. That position alone can activate memories for people who experienced abuse or medical trauma. You're vulnerable. You can't see what's happening. Someone has physical control over your body. Even if they're the kindest person in the world, your nervous system might not care.

The pressure trigger. Deep tissue work, myofascial release, any modality that involves sustained pressure. For some people, that pressure feels grounding. For others, it feels exactly like the weight of an abuser's hand. Your body doesn't distinguish between therapeutic pressure and threatening pressure. It just feels pressure and decides if it's safe.

The breath trigger. Many bodyworkers will ask you to breathe deeply. They'll put their hands on your diaphragm or your ribs. If you've experienced any kind of suffocation, strangulation, or breathing-related trauma, this can be catastrophic. Someone asking you to breathe in a specific way while touching your chest can send you straight into a flashback.

The unexpected sensation trigger. Sometimes it's not even the touch itself. It's the sensation that comes after. The tingling. The heat. The muscle release that feels like something letting go. For a traumatized nervous system, any intense sensation can feel like a loss of control. And loss of control is terrifying.

I remember one time a bodyworker pressed on a spot in my shoulder and I suddenly felt like I was drowning. I wasn't near water. I wasn't thinking about water. But my body remembered something my mind had no access to. That's how flashbacks work in the body. They bypass your thinking brain entirely.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk explains this exact phenomenon. He describes how the body stores trauma in ways that words can't reach. And when you touch those storage sites, the trauma comes out. Not as a memory you can narrate. As a sensation you have to survive.

What to do when it happens (the practical stuff nobody tells you)

Okay. So you're in the middle of bodywork and you feel the flashback coming. Or maybe you're reading this because it already happened and you're trying to figure out what went wrong. Either way, here's what you need to know.

First, you have the right to stop at any time. I cannot say this loudly enough. You can say "stop" or "pause" or "I need to sit up." You do not need to explain yourself. You do not need to apologize. You are allowed to end the session. Full stop.

Second, orient yourself. This is a technique from Dr. Peter Levine's somatic experiencing work. Look around the room. Find five things you can see. Name them out loud or in your head. "I see the lamp. I see the plant. I see the door." This helps your brain remember where you are. You're not in the past. You're in a room. With a lamp. And a plant. And a door you can walk through.

Third, move if you need to. Flashbacks often freeze the body. But movement can help you thaw. Try wiggling your fingers and toes. Shift your position. If you're on a table, ask to sit up. If you're on a mat, roll onto your side. Movement tells your nervous system that you're not trapped.

Fourth, use your voice. Even if it's just a small sound. Even if it's "I'm here" or "this is now." Sound can help you reconnect with your body in a way that silence can't. Your voice is yours. It belongs to you.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). It's not a cure for flashbacks, obviously. But magnesium glycinate is known for its calming effects on the nervous system. Some people find it helps regulate their system after a difficult session. Talk to your doctor before trying anything new, of course.

Fifth, and this is crucial, do not force yourself to continue processing in that moment. Some bodyworkers will tell you to "breathe through it" or "stay with the sensation." That might work for someone with a regulated nervous system. For you, right now, it might make things worse. You are allowed to stop. You are allowed to close the door on that sensation and come back to it later or never. Your safety comes first.

How to find bodyworkers who actually understand trauma

Not all bodywork is the same. And not all bodyworkers are equipped to work with trauma survivors. Here's what to look for.

Ask about their training before you book. Some bodyworkers have specific training in trauma-informed care. They might mention somatic experiencing, sensorimotor psychotherapy, or trauma-sensitive yoga. If they don't mention trauma at all, ask directly: "Do you have experience working with people who have trauma histories?" Pay attention to how they respond. If they seem confused or dismissive, that's your answer.

Look for people who offer choices. A trauma-informed bodyworker will ask about your preferences. They might ask if you want to be face up or face down. They might ask about pressure. They might tell you what they're going to do before they do it. They will invite you to give feedback throughout the session. If someone just starts working without explanation, that's a red flag.

Find people who understand the window of tolerance. This is a concept from Dr. Dan Siegel. The window of tolerance is the zone where your nervous system can handle sensation without flipping into hyperarousal (panic, rage) or hypoarousal (numbness, collapse). A good bodyworker will help you stay within that window. They won't push you past it. They'll slow down if you start to dysregulate.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. It explains how trauma gets stored in the body and how gentle bodywork can help release it. But it also emphasizes the importance of pendulation moving between sensation and safety rather than diving straight into the trauma.

Consider starting with less invasive work. You don't have to start with deep tissue massage. You can start with something gentler. Craniosacral therapy, gentle Thai massage, or even just lying on a mat while someone places their hands lightly on your body. You can also try self-bodywork first. Foam rolling with your own pressure. Stretching in your own space. You get to decide how much touch you can handle.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers concrete exercises for working with your nervous system. Things you can do before and after bodywork to help your system feel safer.

This is not a failure. This is information.

I know it feels awful when bodywork triggers a flashback. It feels like you're broken. Like your body is betraying you. Like you can't even do the things that are supposed to help.

But here's what I've learned. That flashback is not a sign that you're doing something wrong. It's a sign that your body is working exactly as it learned to work. Your body learned that touch can be dangerous. And it's trying to protect you from that danger. The problem is not your body. The problem is that your body's alarm system is calibrated for a threat that no longer exists in the same way.

You can recalibrate that system. But it takes time. And it takes gentleness. And it takes finding people who understand that your body has its own timeline.

I'm not going to tell you that bodywork will eventually feel easy. For some people it does. For others, it always requires some level of negotiation with their nervous system. That's okay. You don't have to love bodywork. You don't have to do it at all. You get to decide what healing looks like for you.

And if you try bodywork again, I hope you find someone who understands. Someone who doesn't push. Someone who lets you keep your eyes open. Someone who asks before they touch. Someone who doesn't take it personally when you say stop.

You deserve that. Your body deserves that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link) - A supplement that may support nervous system regulation
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's essential book on trauma and the body
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's guide to healing trauma through the body
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical workbook for nervous system regulation
